steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank closes Alfred Nobel's quoted phrase and must also connect it to the independent clause that follows: "in 1952, for instance, judges recognized..." That following clause has its own subject and verb, making it a second independent clause that cannot be glued on with a comma alone.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘direction" and,’: Inserting and, after the closing quotation mark creates a grammatical tangle — the coordinating conjunction and cannot be followed immediately by a comma here, and the construction is awkward. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘direction"’: No punctuation after the closing quotation mark leaves two independent clauses fused together with no separator (a run-on). ✗
  • Choice C — ‘direction,"’: A comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice, which violates Standard English conventions. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘direction";’: A semicolon correctly joins two independent clauses — it closes the quotation and links the concrete 1952 example to the definition that preceded it. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
